title: Child Health across the Rural-Urban Spectrum
markdown:
By **Hannah Ameye** and **Joachim De Weerdt**, 2023

**_Topic:**_ Health 

**_Keywords:**_ Nutrition; Child health; Stunting; Urbanisation; East and Southern Africa

**_Africapolis data:**_ GIS, agglomeration data

**_Link to the original article:**_ [Child Health across the Rural-Urban Spectrum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0305750X20300760)

___

Is urban life good or bad for children’s health? And are certain urban areas more conducive to child health than others? In Africa the rural-urban gap in health has been extensively documented and clearly falls in favour of urban areas. But in this literature urbanisation is usually observed and analysed as a binary construct, with a simple rural-urban classification. **We know little about how child health varies across the urban-rural continuum**.  

The relationship between urbanisation and child health is, *a priori*, not obvious. While incomes tend to be higher in larger urban areas, parents may also spend less time at home, spend more time commuting or working, and there may be higher consumption of processed and prepared foods. **There are also large differences in supply-side environments between rural and urban regions, and across various types of urban centres**, with diverging access to water, sanitation and healthcare. Healthcare and other social facilities are generally more readily available in urban regions, but the soar in urbanisation in sub-Saharan Africa may not have been given adequate time to provide sufficient sanitation and livelihood opportunities. Clean water and appropriate waste disposal also experience further pressures from a rapidly growing urban population. **There is still no consensus on how cities of various magnitudes impact health institutions and sanitation**, and by extension child health. 

Our paper sets out to investigate this by **linking [_Demographic and Health Survey (DHS)_](https://dhsprogram.com/) data on 40 000 children from 10 African countries** to [**satellite nightlights**](https://eogdata.mines.edu/products/vnl/) and [**Africapolis population data**](https://africapolis.org/en/data?country=Angola&keyfigure=totalPop&type=abs&year=2015) (Map 1) to provide a plausible continuous measure of the degree of urbanisation, which is consistent across different countries (something which administrative definitions cannot hope to do).  

#####*Map 1. Nighttime lights and urban agglomerations for the 10 countries in our study* 

![img](/images/tempimages/DeWeerdt_1_EN.jpg)


**We find that child stunting, first improves and then worsens at the highest levels of nighttime light** (Figure 2). Child stunting decreases with nighttime lights on the darker left-hand side of the distribution, but then increases again on the brighter right-hand side of the distribution, creating a _U-shaped evolution_. The two vertical lines show the _99th percentile cut-off values_ for rural areas and big towns, beyond which we can be sure there are virtually no rural areas and no big or small towns, respectively. We see that the downward sloping part of the graph occurs at nighttime light values typical in rural areas, the lowest levels occur at nighttime light values typical for towns and some city areas, while **the upward sloping section of the graph is restricted to nighttime lights values that occur only in large cities**. Underlying these average trends across the 10 countries are two types of countries: those for which the improvements level off in the most urbanized locations, and those for which they deteriorate there.

#####*Figure 1. Polynomial association between the probability a child is stunted and nighttime lights* 

![img](/images/tempimages/DeWeerdt_2_EN.jpg)

We then look at plausible mechanisms through which these effects take place. **Sanitation and healthcare access consistently improve with nighttime lights**, which likely contributes to the **decrease in child stunting** but **does not explain the upturn at the highest levels of urbanisation**. However, **child feeding practices**, such as meal frequency, consumption of iron-rich foods and diet diversity, follow both the initial improvement and the subsequent deterioration of _Figure 2_ quite closely - and **are therefore likely proximate causes**. Interestingly, while **children from the richest quartile have the lowest absolute levels of stunting** overall, their stunting rates are the **most sensitive to the level of urbanization at the location they reside**, dropping from _27% in darkest areas_ to _22% in medium-lit areas_, but then rising sharply above _30% in brightest areas_, where urbanization levels are more typical of large cities. 

*The views, conclusions and materials of external contributors do not represent the views of OECD/SWAC or its Members.*

###*Authors' biographies*

[**Hannah Ameye**] (https://sites.google.com/view/hannahameye) is a post-doctoral researcher at [LICOS, KU Leuven] (https://feb.kuleuven.be/drc/licos) specializing in agricultural and development economics. 

[**Joachim De Weerdt**] (https://www.ifpri.org/profile/joachim-de-weerdt)
is a development economist based in Lilongwe, where he heads the Malawi office of the [International Food Policy Research Institute] (https://www.ifpri.org/) (IFPRI)
